FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, 21 sites, children aged 9-10 at baseline (2016-2018), ABCD 5.1 release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using random-intercept cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPM) on ABCD longitudinal data across 4 waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in JAMA Network Open (May 2025); no duplicate flag identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: time spent on social media (IV) paired with depressive symptoms/CBCL score (individual-trait DV). Cross-lagged path: social media use Y1 -> depressive symptoms Y2, β=0.07 (95% CI 0.01-0.12, p=.01); Y2->Y3, β=0.09 (95% CI 0.04-0.14, p<.001). Reverse path (depression->SM) also modeled, yielding β=0.00 at both intervals. Both sides qualify: time on SM is a phone/SM variable; depressive symptoms (CBCL) is an individual-trait outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple quantitative estimates reported: standardized β path coefficients, 95% CIs, p-values for all cross-lagged and autoregressive paths in main Table 2 / Figure 2 and eTable 3 (Supplement 1).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis (RI-CLPM structural equation model) of SM use <-> depressive symptoms with numeric results throughout.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal, but SM appears as both IV (SM->depression cross-lagged paths) and DV (depression->SM cross-lagged paths) in a bidirectional RI-CLPM. SM is IV in at least one analysis; FT-EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis with no SM-as-IV analysis anywhere. Condition (c) is not met.
notes: Bidirectional RI-CLPM; social media use is both predictor and outcome. Cross-lagged effect of SM on depression is significant at years 1-2 and 2-3; reverse path is not significant. N=11,876 ABCD participants.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, nationwide U.S. sample, 11,875 adolescents ages 9-10 at baseline (2016-2018), ABCD 5.0 release.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: multivariable linear regression (and mediation models) predicting manic symptoms from screen-time subtypes using ABCD prospective cohort data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Social Psychiatry and Psychiatric Epidemiology (2025); no duplicate flag identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 hit: social media screen time (Year 1) as IV paired with manic symptoms (Year 3, 7 Up Mania scale) as individual-trait DV. Adjusted coefficient B=0.20 (95% CI 0.09, 0.32, p=0.001) in Table 2. Texting screen time also qualifies: B=0.18 (95% CI 0.08, 0.28, p<0.001). Both sides qualify: time on social media/texting are phone/SM-specific variables; manic symptoms are an individual-trait (psychiatric symptom) outcome.

FT_IC3.evidence: Regression coefficients (B) with 95% CIs and p-values reported for all screen subtypes in Table 2. Mediation indirect-effect estimates (B with bootstrapped CIs) in Figures 1-3 and Supplementary Table 3.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential multivariable linear regression and mediation analyses with numeric results for the SM/texting <-> manic symptoms relationship.

FT_EC7_reason: SM (social media screen time) is the IV predicting manic symptoms (DV) in the main prospective analyses. FT-EC7 requires SM to be DV in every analysis; here SM is IV. Condition (b) is not met.
notes: Sensitivity analyses also include cross-sectional associations of screen time with manic symptoms in Year 3 (Supplemental Table 1), further confirming SM-as-IV. SMAQ (problematic social media use) appears only as a mediator, not as the primary SM variable tested against an individual-trait outcome; the qualifying SM variable is time on social media/texting as IV.

FT_IC1.evidence: Cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Study Year 3 follow-up (2019-2021), 5.0 release, 11,876 children recruited from 21 U.S. sites.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional logistic regression estimating associations between transgender/gender-questioning identity and lifetime cyberbullying victimization.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed article in Academic Pediatrics (2025); no duplicate flag identified.

FT_IC2.evidence: The exposure variable is transgender/gender-questioning identity and the outcome is lifetime cyberbullying victimization. Neither is a qualifying phone/SM variable. Step 1: no numeric estimate in any results location (main tables, supplementary appendices A-B) where a phone/SM-specific variable label (time on social media, time on phone, texting, SM platform, SMAQ, MPIQ, etc.) appears as IV or DV with its own coefficient paired with an individual-trait variable on the other side. 'Time spent on the internet (hours per day)' appears only as a covariate in sensitivity models (Supplementary Appendix A), not as an IV or DV with its own effect estimate. Step 2 does not apply (no composite to assess). Per binding clarification 3: cyberbullying alone does not qualify as a phone/SM variable. FT-IC2 = NOT_MET.

notes: Paper studies transgender/gender-questioning identity (IV) -> cyberbullying victimization (DV). Cyberbullying alone fails IC2 per binding clarification. Internet time adjusted for as covariate only, no standalone SM/phone coefficient. Exclusion code: FT-EC2.
